subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940)
stimulus:
Newton Cantwell and his sons, who were Jehovah's Witnesses, went door-to-door in a New Haven, Connecticut, neighborhood to distribute pamphlets about their religion and encouraged people to join their church. The men did not have a permit to solicit door-to-door. A Connecticut state court convicted the Cantwells of violating an ordinance that stated "[n]o person shall solicit money, services, subscriptions or any valuable thing for any alleged religious, charitable or philanthropic cause. . . unless such cause shall have been approved by the secretary of the public welfare council."

Cantwell and his sons challenged the conviction, arguing that they did not need to obtain a permit because their activities were protected by the United States Constitution. The Connecticut Supreme Court upheld the conviction, finding that the statute was in the public interest and that it protected against fraud. The Cantwells appealed to the United States Supreme Court.

In a unanimous decision, the Supreme Court ruled in the Cantwells' favor, holding that the United States Constitution prohibited states from making laws like the one in question in New Haven. The Court reasoned that the First Amendment gives citizens the right to believe, as well as the right to act on those beliefs, and regarding the right to act, laws may not deny or limit the right to preach or disseminate religious views.

parts.prompt: Identify the First Amendment clause that is common to both Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) and Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the free exercise clause earns the point. The establishment clause does not earn the point for this question.

scoring_points.description: Identify the First Amendment clause that is common to both Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) and Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940).

required_elements: Identifies the free exercise clause as the First Amendment clause common to both cases
alternatives.correct_example: The free exercise clause of the First Amendment is common to both Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut.

wrong_examples: The establishment clause — neither case involved government-sponsored religion; both involved individuals exercising their faith.
Freedom of speech — while religious expression has speech elements, the specific First Amendment clause at stake is the free exercise clause.
common_traps: Do not accept the establishment clause — the Cantwells were being restricted from practicing their religion, not the government establishing one
Accept 'free exercise of religion' or equivalent phrasing
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the First Amendment clause that is common to both Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) and Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940). Acceptable response: The free exercise clause.

sample_responses.response: The free exercise clause of the First Amendment is common to both Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the free exercise clause as the common First Amendment clause in both cases. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Both cases involve the establishment clause.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect — neither case involved government establishment of religion. Both involved individuals seeking to exercise their religious beliefs free from government restriction. The relevant clause is free exercise, not establishment. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut resulted in the Supreme Court issuing similar holdings in both cases.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate description of Wisconsin v. Yoder (the required case). Second point requires connecting both cases through the common thread — in both, government laws restricted religious practice, and in both the Court held the free exercise clause protected the religious conduct from that restriction.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut resulted in similar holdings. (1 point — describe relevant fact or holding about the required Supreme Court case Wisconsin v. Yoder)

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from Wisconsin v. Yoder, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: In Wisconsin v. Yoder, Amish parents refused to send their children to public school past the eighth grade because they believed doing so would violate their religious way of life.

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from Wisconsin v. Yoder, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: The Supreme Court held in Yoder that exempting Amish children from Wisconsin's compulsory school attendance law was required by the free exercise clause because the law unconstitutionally burdened their religious practice.

wrong_examples: Describes only the Cantwell facts without mentioning Yoder — this earns 0 points.
Confuses Yoder with Engel v. Vitale or another religion case.
common_traps: Only information about Yoder is needed for the first point — a full comparison is not yet required
Both a factual description and a description of the holding earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point for describing relevant information (facts or holding) about the required Supreme Court case Wisconsin v. Yoder. Acceptable responses: In Yoder, mandatory public school attendance beyond the eighth grade was contrary to the religious beliefs of some Amish parents. In Yoder, the Court held that exemptions from school attendance requirements for religious students were protected by the free exercise clause.

sample_responses.response: In Wisconsin v. Yoder, Amish parents refused to send their children to public school past the eighth grade because they believed doing so would violate their religious way of life. The Supreme Court held that exempting these students from the compulsory attendance law was required by the free exercise clause.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes both facts and the holding from Yoder — the required case. Earns the first point.

sample_responses.response: In Cantwell, Jehovah's Witnesses were arrested for distributing religious pamphlets without a permit.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes Cantwell, not the required case Yoder. The first point requires information about Wisconsin v. Yoder. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Wisconsin v. Yoder and Cantwell v. Connecticut resulted in similar holdings. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to similar holdings)

required_elements: Explains how the facts of both cases involved government restrictions on religious practice, and how both led the Court to rule in favor of the religious parties under the free exercise clause
alternatives.correct_example: In Yoder, Wisconsin's school attendance law forced Amish families to violate their religious beliefs by requiring schooling past eighth grade. In Cantwell, Connecticut's permit requirement forced Jehovah's Witnesses to seek government approval before engaging in religious solicitation. In both cases, the Court held that the government could not enforce laws that burdened the free exercise of religion, leading to similar holdings in favor of the religious parties.

wrong_examples: Describes the facts of only one case — earns 1 point (b1), not both.
States that 'both cases involved religion' without explaining how the specific facts led to the similar outcomes.
common_traps: Must address BOTH cases to earn the second point
The key similarity is that both involved government laws restricting religious practice, and both courts ruled the free exercise clause protected the conduct
scoring_points.official_rubric: Two points for correctly explaining how the facts in both cases resulted in the Supreme Court issuing similar holdings. Acceptable responses: While Yoder was about school attendance and Cantwell was about solicitation, both cases concerned legal restrictions on religious practice. In both cases, the Court held that the laws were unconstitutional because they violated the free exercise clause. In Yoder, parents claimed that a law requiring school past the eighth grade violated their religious beliefs. The Court held that exemptions from school for religious students were protected by the free exercise clause. In Cantwell, Jehovah's Witnesses claimed that regulations on door-to-door solicitation were a restriction on a religious practice. The Court held that their solicitation, even without a permit, was also protected by the free exercise clause.

sample_responses.response: In Yoder, Wisconsin's compulsory school attendance law forced Amish families to violate their religious beliefs. In Cantwell, Connecticut's permit requirement for solicitation restricted Jehovah's Witnesses' religious practice. In both cases, the Supreme Court held that the government could not enforce laws that burdened the free exercise of religion — ruling in favor of the religious parties in similar holdings.
sample_responses.commentary: Addresses both cases and explains how the specific facts of each led to the common holding — government restriction on religious practice ruled unconstitutional under the free exercise clause. Earns both points.

sample_responses.response: Both cases involved religious freedom and the First Amendment.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — names the theme but does not explain how the specific facts of each case led to the similar holdings. Does not earn the second point. 0 pts for b2.

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts of Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) illustrate the Court's need to balance government power and the rights of citizens.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must identify both the government's legitimate interest (regulating solicitation/preventing fraud) and the individual right at stake (free exercise of religion) and explain how the Court weighed these competing interests.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts of Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) illustrate the Court's need to balance government power and the rights of citizens.

required_elements: Identifies both the government interest (regulating solicitation to protect against fraud) and the individual right (free exercise of religion)
Explains that the Court had to weigh these competing interests
alternatives.correct_example: In Cantwell, Connecticut argued it had a legitimate interest in preventing fraudulent charitable solicitation, which is a proper exercise of government power. But the Cantwells had a First Amendment right to spread their religious beliefs. The Court had to balance these competing interests and ultimately concluded that the right to free exercise outweighed the state's interest in requiring a permit for religious solicitation.

required_elements: Explains that the case required the Court to weigh government regulation against individual religious freedom
alternatives.correct_example: The Cantwell case illustrates the tension between government power and individual rights — Connecticut's law served a real public interest in protecting citizens from fraud, while the Cantwells had a constitutionally protected right to practice and spread their religion. The Court's task was to determine which interest should prevail, demonstrating that neither government power nor individual rights is absolute.

wrong_examples: Simply stating that the Court ruled in favor of Cantwell — does not address the balance between competing interests.
Mentioning only the individual's rights without acknowledging the government's legitimate interest.
common_traps: A complete answer requires identifying BOTH the government interest and the individual right — one alone is insufficient
The response must explain the tension/balance, not just restate who won
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the facts of Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) illustrate the Court's need to balance government power and the rights of citizens. Acceptable explanations include: In Cantwell, the Court had to balance the government's power to regulate door-to-door solicitation with the right of citizens to freely practice their religion.

sample_responses.response: In Cantwell, Connecticut argued it had a legitimate government interest in protecting citizens from fraudulent charitable solicitation — a proper exercise of state police power. But the Cantwells had a First Amendment right to spread their religious beliefs through solicitation. The Court had to balance these competing interests and ultimately held that the right to free exercise outweighed the state's interest in requiring a permit.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies both the government interest (preventing fraud) and the individual right (free exercise), and explains the Court's balancing act. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The Court ruled in favor of the Cantwells because their rights were violated.
sample_responses.commentary: States the outcome but does not explain the balancing of government power against individual rights — does not identify the government's legitimate interest or explain the tension between the two. 0 pts.
